metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the backend is threaded into `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch`: either (a) move `_load_reconcile_backend()` before the probe dispatch call in `run_differs()` and add `backend` to the dispatch function's signature, or (b) note in the Approach that the backend load is reordered to precede probe dispatch.
evidence: Plan: 'run_differs.py:369 routes through the backend: isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) guarded'

Code: run_differs() calls _run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch(mutations, route_inbound_probe) at line 230, then backend = _load_reconcile_backend() at line 234 — backend doesn't exist yet at probe dispatch time
_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch signature: def _run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch(mutations, route_inbound_probe) -> None — no backend parameter
findings.finding: The `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` function currently takes no `backend` parameter, and in `run_differs()` the backend is loaded AFTER this function is called (line 230 dispatches probe, line 234 loads the backend). The plan states `run_differs.py:369` will be `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)`-guarded, but does not explain how the backend object is threaded into (or made available before) the probe dispatch. Without addressing this ordering/wiring gap, the implementer will reach the capability check with no backend in scope.
findings.location: Approach section / run_differs.py:204-240 (run_differs function body ordering)
scenarios: Implementer adds isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) check inside _run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch but backend is not in scope — NameError at runtime
Implementer moves backend load before probe dispatch without noticing the reorder changes the observable call sequence (backend config loaded even when no probe mutations exist)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'In `run_differs()`, move `backend = _load_reconcile_backend()` to before the `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` call, and thread `backend` into that function as a third parameter so the `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)` guard has the backend in scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_load_reconcile_backend()` is called before `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` (or that `backend` is passed as a new parameter to it) so the `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)` guard is structurally reachable at the probe call site.
evidence: Plan: 'run_differs.py:369 routes through the backend: isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) guarded'
run_differs.py:230: `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch(mutations, route_inbound_probe)` — no backend argument
run_differs.py:234: `backend = _load_reconcile_backend()` — backend obtained AFTER the probe dispatch call
The plan's Scope section lists run_differs.py as a file to modify but does not explain how backend will be made available to the probe dispatch phase
findings.finding: The plan asserts `run_differs.py:369` will be rewired to route through `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)`, but in the current code the `backend` object is obtained at line 234 (via `_load_reconcile_backend()`) AFTER `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` is called at line 230. The plan does not specify that the backend acquisition will be moved earlier, nor that `backend` will be passed into `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch`.
scenarios: Implementer follows the plan literally, tries to add isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) inside _run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch but has no backend object — either the call ordering must change or a new parameter must be added, neither of which is specified in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: '`run_differs.py` must acquire the backend (via `_load_reconcile_backend()`) before the `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` call, and pass it as a new `backend` parameter to that helper. The current code acquires the backend at line 234, after the probe dispatch at line 230 — swap the ordering and update the helper signature.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `backend` is made available to `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` — either document that `_load_reconcile_backend()` is moved above line 230 in `run_differs.py`, or that `backend` is added as a parameter to `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch`, so the `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)` guard can be evaluated at the probe call site.
evidence: run_differs.py line 230: `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch(mutations, route_inbound_probe)` — before backend is loaded
run_differs.py line 234: `backend = _load_reconcile_backend()` — after probe dispatch
Plan: 'run_differs.py:369 routes through the backend: isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) guarded'
ABSENCE: plan has no mention of reordering the backend load or threading backend into _run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch
findings.finding: In run_differs.py, `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch(mutations, route_inbound_probe)` is called at line 230, but `backend = _load_reconcile_backend()` is loaded at line 234 — AFTER the probe dispatch. The plan says `run_differs.py:369` should route through `isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe)`, but the backend object is not available at the probe dispatch call site without either reordering the backend load to before line 230 or adding `backend` as an argument to `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch`. The plan does not acknowledge this ordering constraint or specify how the backend is threaded in.
scenarios: Implementer rewires probe dispatch to use SupportsAbsenceProbe but has no backend object in scope at call time — the isinstance guard cannot be evaluated.
Implementer moves backend load before line 230 without noting it changes outbound/inbound differ phases' backend reference — subtle ordering side-effect.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `backend = _load_reconcile_backend()` to before the `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch` call (line 230), and pass `backend` as an additional argument to `_run_differs_inbound_probe_dispatch`. Update `run_differs.py` scope note to reflect this signature change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC2/AC3) explicitly requiring that JiraBackend satisfies isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) and that the happy-path/failure probe tests in adapters/jira/probe.py exercise it end-to-end through JiraBackend.probe_remote.
evidence: 'with a FakeBackend implementing SupportsAbsenceProbe, run_differs uses the backend's probe (runtime proof)' — FakeBackend only, no mention of JiraBackend in the routing tests

'adapters/jira/backend.py (implement probe_remote)' is listed in Scope but no AC requires verifying JiraBackend isinstance-satisfies SupportsAbsenceProbe or that its probe_remote exercises the real adapter/jira/probe.py
findings.finding: AC2 verifies capability routing using a FakeBackend but does not require verifying that the real JiraBackend in adapters/jira/backend.py actually implements probe_remote — the Jira implementation (a Scope deliverable) has no dedicated acceptance criterion.
scenarios: JiraBackend.probe_remote is stubbed or missing; all FakeBackend routing tests pass; the real Jira probe path is broken in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'JiraBackend satisfies isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) — verified by at least one migrated probe test calling backend.probe_remote() through the real JiraBackend (not FakeBackend).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) verifying that the original test files (tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/test_inbound_probe.py and test_inbound_probe_dispatch.py) are deleted — e.g., assert their paths do not exist in the repo or that make test collects zero tests from those paths.
evidence: Scope says 'migrate to the adapter home' implying removal of originals, but no AC asserts old paths are deleted or no longer collected
E1 rubric: 'for migrations, criteria verify BOTH removal and replacement'
AC3 says only 'verified by the migrated probe tests' — verifies replacement only
findings.finding: The test migration (old test files moved from tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/ to the adapter home) has no AC verifying deletion of the original files; only replacement is verified, not removal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs); Scope: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/test_inbound_probe.py, test_inbound_probe_dispatch.py
scenarios: Old test files remain at their original paths, collecting duplicate tests, creating ambiguity about canonical test location, and potentially failing CI de-duplication checks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the standalone urllib GET in the planned `adapters/jira/probe.py` with a call to `self.transport._direct_rest_get('/rest/api/2/issue/{key}?fields=status,resolution')` (available via `AcliRestMixin` on `AcliClient`) to avoid re-implementing credential encoding, request construction, and retry logic that already exist in `acli_rest.py`.
evidence: acli_rest.py:207-229: _direct_rest_get builds exactly: url from jira_url+path, base64 basic-auth header, urllib.request.Request(method='GET'), urlopen with retry, json.loads decode — identical to what inbound_probe.py:50-59 does today

acli_rest.py:36-83: _rest_urlopen_with_retry already provides 3-attempt retry with 2s/5s backoff for TimeoutError/ConnectionError/URLError — the plan's probe.py would duplicate this retry logic or omit it (a regression)

inbound_probe.py:50-59: _make_request builds the same base64 auth header and urllib.request.Request as _direct_rest_get — the duplication already exists and the plan perpetuates it rather than eliminating it
JiraBackend.__init__ sets self.transport = transport (an AcliClient), making _direct_rest_get accessible via self.transport._direct_rest_get(...)
findings.finding: adapters/jira/probe.py will re-implement basic-auth urllib GET (URL construction, base64 credential encoding, Request object, urlopen, JSON decode) that already exists verbatim in AcliRestMixin._direct_rest_get (acli_rest.py:207-229) and AcliRestMixin._rest_urlopen_with_retry (acli_rest.py:36-83). Since JiraBackend.transport IS an AcliClient (which mixes in AcliRestMixin), probe.py can reuse transport._direct_rest_get('/rest/api/2/issue/{key}?fields=status,resolution') instead of rebuilding the same urllib pattern.
findings.location: Approach section ('Jira mechanics (URL construction, basic-auth GET …) move to adapters/jira/probe.py'); adapters/jira/acli_rest.py:207-229
scenarios: probe.py's standalone urlopen call lacks the retry-with-backoff from _rest_urlopen_with_retry, making the probe more fragile than all other Jira REST calls.
A future change to credential encoding or auth header format must be made in two places (AcliRestMixin and probe.py) instead of one.
findings.suggested_fix: In adapters/jira/probe.py, accept the transport (AcliClient) as a parameter and call `transport._direct_rest_get(f'/rest/api/2/issue/{issue_key}?fields=status,resolution')`. Catch urllib.error.HTTPError and urllib.error.URLError/TimeoutError as the existing probe does. This eliminates _make_request, the base64 import, and the standalone urlopen — leaving only the status-name classification logic unique to probe.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the Approach or Scope section what value `run_differs.py` passes as `remote_id` (e.g., 'the Jira issue key string already extracted at the existing probe call site').
evidence: Plan text: 'probe_remote(remote_id: str) -> Any'
Plan text: 'run_differs.py:369 routes through the backend: isinstance(backend, SupportsAbsenceProbe) guarded'
ABSENCE: no statement of what argument value (e.g., the Jira issue key extracted from the inbound mutation) is passed as remote_id at the call site
findings.finding: The `probe_remote(remote_id: str)` signature in `SupportsAbsenceProbe` never states what value is passed as `remote_id` at the `run_differs.py:369` call site — an executor must reverse-engineer this from the existing code rather than reading it from the plan.
findings.location: Approach section — `SupportsAbsenceProbe` protocol definition; run_differs.py:369 call site
scenarios: An implementing agent writes probe_remote to accept a ticket URL instead of an issue key, or passes the wrong field from run_differs.py, producing silent classification errors.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach: 'At the call site in run_differs.py, `remote_id` is the Jira issue key string (e.g. "PROJ-123") already available at line 369; the adapter's probe_remote constructs the REST URL from it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback note or expand-contract sequence — e.g., 'keep the old probe function in inbound_probe.py under a deprecation shim until AC3 (byte-identical behavior) is confirmed green, then delete it in a follow-up commit' — or state that a single-commit revert of this PR fully restores the prior behavior.
evidence: 'The Jira mechanics ... move to a new adapters/jira/probe.py' — the existing probe function is deleted from inbound_probe.py with no back-out mechanism described
'run_differs.py:369 routes through the backend' replaces a direct call — no expand-contract (e.g., keep old path temporarily behind a flag) or revert instructions mentioned
T4 rubric: 'acknowledgement alone is insufficient; require the reversibility mechanism'
findings.finding: The plan removes the probe function from inbound_probe.py and changes run_differs.py:369's call pattern, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence for this behavioral change.
scenarios: The refactoring introduces a subtle regression (e.g., env resolution order differs in the adapter); with no rollback plan, reverting requires manually reconstructing the original inbound_probe.py probe code from history.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'This change is fully reversible by reverting the single PR/commit; no data migrations or external state changes are involved. An expand-contract sequence is not required because the test suite (AC3) provides the behavior-identity gate before the old code is deleted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR entry (or update ADR 0035 / AGENTS.md) documenting the `SupportsAbsenceProbe` capability protocol — its contract, the fallback-to-UNREACHABLE policy, and the pattern for future backends to implement.
evidence: Plan cites 'ADR 0035 context list' as the authority for location-pinning inbound_probe.py, confirming ADRs are the project's record of architectural decisions.
No mention of a new ADR or update to AGENTS.md / design docs for SupportsAbsenceProbe.

The plan says this is 'consistent with existing capability pattern' but the existing patterns (SupportsLinks etc.) presumably already have docs; adding a fourth capability pattern still constitutes an architectural decision worth recording.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `SupportsAbsenceProbe` as a new backend capability Protocol — a new architectural decision at the engine/adapter boundary — but mentions no ADR creation or update to capture this decision, even though the project already has an ADR system (ADR 0035 is cited).
scenarios: A future developer adding a third backend must discover SupportsAbsenceProbe by reading source rather than ADRs, risking inconsistent implementation.
If ADR 0035 enumerates all capability protocols (SupportsLinks etc.), it becomes stale immediately after this change lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a short ADR section (or append to ADR 0035) covering: (1) motivation for SupportsAbsenceProbe, (2) probe_remote signature and ProbeResult contract, (3) the capability-absent fallback (UNREACHABLE), (4) the rule that all vendor-specific probe mechanics must live under adapters/<vendor>/.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact destination path for the migrated test files and audit CI configs / AGENTS.md / any test-path index for references to `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/test_inbound_probe*.py` that need updating.
evidence: Scope says 'migrate to the adapter home' without specifying the destination directory.
No mention of updating CI config, AGENTS.md, or any file-path reference that currently points to tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/.
Plan says inbound_probe.py itself is location-pinned by ADR 0035, suggesting location metadata is actively tracked — test paths may be similarly tracked.
findings.finding: The plan moves two test files from `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/` to an unspecified 'adapter home' without stating the destination path or checking for stale references in CI configs, AGENTS.md, or any test-path index that might reference the old location.
findings.location: Scope section — 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/fetch/test_inbound_probe.py, test_inbound_probe_dispatch.py (migrate to the adapter home)'
scenarios: A CI job or AGENTS.md entry that names the old test path continues to reference a deleted file, causing collection errors or misleading docs even though make test still passes (pytest discovers by pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or implementation note to `adapters/jira/probe.py` specifying that the HTTP GET must be issued with an explicit timeout (e.g. `timeout=10` seconds), and add a test asserting that a simulated slow response (mock that sleeps/raises `socket.timeout`) is classified as UNREACHABLE.
evidence: Plan lists failure modes: '5xx/401/timeout/URLError → UNREACHABLE' but no AC or implementation note mandates a timeout be set on urllib/requests call
No AC or scope note specifies a timeout value or that `timeout=` must be passed to the HTTP client
findings.finding: The plan lists 'timeout' as a handled failure mode (→ UNREACHABLE) but never specifies that a concrete HTTP timeout is actually configured on the Jira GET in `adapters/jira/probe.py`. Without an explicit timeout value requirement, an implementation could omit it, causing the synchronous GET to block the reconciler thread indefinitely on a slow/hung Jira endpoint.
scenarios: Jira REST endpoint hangs without responding; reconciler thread blocks indefinitely
Network partition causes TCP connection to stall; no timeout means the probe never resolves to UNREACHABLE
findings.suggested_fix: In `adapters/jira/probe.py`, pass `timeout=<N>` to the HTTP call (e.g. `urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10)`). Make the timeout configurable via an env var (e.g. `JIRA_PROBE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`, defaulting to 10). Add one AC line: 'Jira GET is issued with a finite timeout; a simulated hung connection is classified UNREACHABLE within that bound.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement (at minimum a structured log line per probe outcome, including branch and any exception type) to the `adapters/jira/probe.py` spec and add a corresponding AC verifying that a logger call is emitted on failure paths (UNREACHABLE, ProbeConfigError).
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, or tracing in the plan, scope, or ACs
Plan's ACs cover correctness and neutrality but not operational observability of the new external integration point
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new external Jira REST call path (`adapters/jira/probe.py`) but specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or tracing) for probe outcomes or failures. Operators have no visibility into probe failure rates, classification distributions, or Jira API health without this.
findings.location: adapters/jira/probe.py (new file); Testing section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Jira API becomes flaky and starts returning 5xx; reconciler silently degrades to UNREACHABLE for all probes with no alert or log trail
ProbeConfigError (missing env vars) fires in production; no log record makes root-cause analysis slow
findings.suggested_fix: In `adapters/jira/probe.py`, emit a `logger.warning(...)` or `logger.debug(...)` on each classification branch, including the error type on UNREACHABLE. Add a single AC: 'Each probe outcome (branch name, remote_id) is logged at DEBUG; failures (UNREACHABLE, ProbeConfigError) are logged at WARNING with the exception detail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `SupportsAbsenceProbe` satisfies Rule-of-Three before introducing the Protocol: either identify ≥2 additional concrete call sites / implementors that exist TODAY, or document why the single-site Protocol is worth the ceremony over a direct `JiraBackend.probe_remote()` call without an isinstance guard (the Protocol can be added when the second backend arrives).
evidence: run_differs.py:369 is the ONLY call site for the probe: `probe_result = probe_mod.probe(_m.target)` — one routing point
JiraBackend is the only adapter that would implement probe_remote; no second backend exists (plan: 'The first real second backend is out of scope here')
SupportsLinks has call sites in test_backend_capability.py (lines 17, 27, 34, 39), SupportsComments (lines 15, 40, 63), SupportsIncremental (lines 16, 69) — multiple grounded uses each

A single-site Protocol is a premature abstraction under Rule-of-Three; the refactoring goal (removing adapters.jira from core) can be achieved without a new Protocol by delegating directly through JiraBackend without an isinstance guard
findings.finding: SupportsAbsenceProbe has exactly one current call site (run_differs.py:369) and one implementor (JiraBackend), failing Rule-of-Three for a new Protocol abstraction. The three existing capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) each have multiple confirmed test/call-site references; SupportsAbsenceProbe does not.
scenarios: A second backend is never actually built, leaving SupportsAbsenceProbe as a single-implementor Protocol in perpetuity.
An implementor adds probe_remote but forgets isinstance guard in run_differs, and the single-Protocol abstraction adds ceremony with no enforcement benefit over a direct call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the TLS posture of the new `adapters/jira/probe.py` outbound path: specify that JIRA_URL must use an `https://` scheme (validate at startup or probe-call time) and that the HTTP client uses certificate verification (the default for `urllib`/`requests`, but should be explicit in the adapter's design notes or an assertion in the code).
evidence: 'The Jira mechanics (URL construction, basic-auth GET, GET-only invariant, status-name classification, JIRA_* env resolution) move to a new adapters/jira/probe.py'
Plan creates a new file that explicitly owns the credentials-bearing HTTP call but states no HTTPS-enforcement or cert-validation requirement
ABSENCE: no mention of HTTPS-only JIRA_URL validation, no mention of SSL certificate verification posture in the new adapter
findings.finding: The new `adapters/jira/probe.py` file — explicitly named in the plan as owning 'URL construction' and 'basic-auth GET' — transmits JIRA_USER and JIRA_API_TOKEN credentials over a network path whose TLS posture is undeclared. The plan does not specify that JIRA_URL must use an HTTPS scheme, nor that the HTTP client enforces certificate validation, leaving the credential transmission posture ambiguous on a boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Jira mechanics (URL construction, basic-auth GET … JIRA_* env resolution) move to adapters/jira/probe.py'; Scope section — 'adapters/jira/probe.py (new: Jira GET + classification)'
scenarios: JIRA_URL is misconfigured (or an attacker-influenced env var) with an http:// scheme; basic-auth credentials are transmitted in plaintext over the network
The HTTP client library (urllib / requests) is invoked with cert verification disabled or defaulting to no-verify in a containerised CI environment, silently accepting a MITM certificate
findings.suggested_fix: In the design notes for `adapters/jira/probe.py`, add: (1) a startup guard that raises `ProbeConfigError` if `JIRA_URL` does not start with `https://`; (2) a note that the HTTP GET uses the stdlib `urllib` (or `requests`) default of `verify=True` and must not override it. Add a test case for the HTTP-scheme rejection to the Testing section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
